pair_convention.spot_explanation: NZD/JPY at 91.50 means 1 NZD buys 91.50 JPY. A rising pair means NZD is strengthening against JPY (more yen per kiwi dollar), while a falling pair means NZD is weakening versus JPY or equivalently JPY is strengthening versus NZD (fewer yen per kiwi dollar).
pair_convention.bearish_means: pair FALLS (NZD weakens vs JPY / JPY strengthens vs NZD)

executive_summary: NZD/JPY trades at 91.50, meaning 1 NZD buys 91.50 JPY, with the pair sitting at the 78th percentile of its 52-week range (79.81–94.86) after a sharp rally from the April 2025 low of 79.81 followed by a recent rollover. The pair has declined 2.38% over the past month and 0.93% over three months, but remains up 4.42% over six months and 4.41% over one year. The dominant bilateral narrative is one of duelling weaknesses: NZD is hobbled by a stagflationary macro backdrop (3.1% CPI, 0.2% GDP, 5.4% unemployment) with the RBNZ paralysed at 2.25%, while JPY suffers from catastrophic terms-of-trade deterioration as the Iran-US war pushes Brent above $107/bbl against Japan's 90% energy import dependency. The critical bilateral edge is that the BoJ is poised to hike to 1.00% on April 27–28 (~70% probability), compressing the NZD-JPY rate differential from 150bp toward 125bp, while the RBNZ remains frozen — creating directional asymmetry favouring NZD/JPY downside. The NZD-JPY carry differential of +150bp produces a carry-to-vol ratio of only ~0.13, well below the 0.40 threshold that attracts aggressive accumulation, confirming this is not a compelling carry trade. Our net stance is Bearish tactically (conviction 3/5) driven by the BoJ hike catalyst and deteriorating NZD fundamentals, and Bearish strategically (conviction 2/5) as the rate convergence thesis confronts offsetting dairy support for NZD and persistent energy headwinds for JPY.

market_context.valuation_assessment: NZD is approximately 14% undervalued versus JPY on a PPP basis (dossier-implied NZD/JPY fair value ~105 from the NZD side; ~65 from the JPY side). The directional signal from PPP is ambiguous: NZD is deeply cheap vs JPY because JPY itself is even more extremely undervalued in REER terms (BIS REER at all-time lows). At current levels, NZD/JPY is roughly consistent with the bilateral fundamentals — neither obviously mispriced. The pair's valuation is dominated by the carry differential and risk appetite rather than PPP mechanics.

positioning.net_pair_implication: Both currencies are net short against USD by speculators, but NZD's positioning is at a more extreme percentile (80th–90th), suggesting greater crowding risk on the NZD leg. The NZD short is near exhaustion while the JPY short has room to build — a divergence that could see NZD covering faster than JPY, pushing NZD/JPY higher on any positive NZD catalyst, but the BoJ hike provides a JPY covering catalyst that would overwhelm any NZD squeeze.
positioning.crowding_assessment: NZD short heavily crowded (80th–90th percentile); JPY short moderate (32nd percentile); net pair implication muted-to-modestly bearish NZD/JPY

pair_technicals.key_moving_averages: 50d SMA: 92.72 (spot below — bearish near-term); 100d SMA: 92.30 (spot below — bearish near-term); 200d SMA: 90.00 (spot above — structural support). Configuration is mixed: spot below shorter MAs signals near-term bearish momentum, but the 200-day provides a clear support floor 150 pips below current levels.
pair_technicals.chart_pattern: Descending triangle on the 4-hour chart with pair approaching breakout conditions. A downside break could target 92.30 (S1), then 91.57 (S2) and 91.02 (S3), while bullish reversal candlesticks could lift toward 93.39 (R1) and 93.94 (R2). Tickmill identifies overlap support at 91.72 with potential bounce toward first resistance at 92.63.

pair_volatility.rv_vs_iv: IV modestly overpricing realised moves — a small volatility risk premium exists. RV at ~8–9% is below IV at ~11–13%, confirming event premium is embedded but not yet realised in spot.
pair_volatility.risk_reversals: Mildly negative skew (NZD/JPY puts bid), consistent with both NZD puts bid against USD and JPY strength hedging demand.
pair_volatility.vol_term_structure: Likely in backwardation around the April 6 Hormuz deadline and April 27–28 BoJ meeting, with front-end vol elevated relative to the back end due to concentrated near-term event risk.
pair_volatility.vol_regime: Moderate / event-elevated — IV at 11–13% with event premium embedded but RV below IV

market_context.recent_regime: NZD/JPY peaked near 94.86 in early February 2026 as the NZD carried a strong dairy-rally tailwind and JPY weakened on energy-shock terms-of-trade deterioration. Since mid-February, the pair has reversed as the Iran conflict deepened, NZ business confidence collapsed (ANZ from 59 to 33), and BoJ hawkish signaling intensified (Takata dissent, Summary of Opinions debate on hike size). The pair's recent price action reflects a tug-of-war between NZD weakness (stagflation, oil vulnerability) and JPY weakness (energy import costs), with JPY starting to gain the upper hand as the BoJ hike approaches.

bilateral_carry_analysis.breakeven_move: Spot can move approximately 115 pips against before carry is erased over 1 month at +150bp differential (91.50 to ~90.35). Post-BoJ hike to 1.00%, carry narrows to +125bp and the breakeven shrinks to ~96 pips.

forward_points.1m: +11 pips (NZD at slight premium from rate differential)

bilateral_carry_analysis.carry_assessment: The RBNZ OCR at 2.25% versus the BoJ rate at 0.75% creates a bilateral policy rate differential of +150bp in favour of NZD, generating positive annualised carry for long NZD/JPY. However, this carry is set to compress: the BoJ is priced at ~70% probability to hike to 1.00% on April 27–28, which would narrow the differential to +125bp, while the RBNZ is expected to hold through at least May. At the estimated 1M implied vol of ~11–12%, the carry-to-vol ratio is approximately 0.13 — well below the 0.40 threshold that attracts aggressive carry accumulation, confirming this is not a compelling carry trade. The carry regime is 'not reliably working' because NZD/JPY is a pure risk-proxy pair where safe-haven JPY rallies in risk-off conditions easily overwhelm the carry advantage — the April 2025 drop from 87 to 79.81 (7.7 big figures) demonstrates that carry provides negligible protection in a risk-off unwind.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The NZD-JPY policy rate differential of +150bp (RBNZ 2.25% vs BoJ 0.75%) is set to narrow by 25bp in the next four weeks as the BoJ hikes to 1.00% on April 27–28 (~70% probability) while the RBNZ holds at the April 8 MPR (~100% hold probability). This is the most asymmetric rates setup in G10 crosses: one central bank is almost certainly hiking (BoJ) while the other is frozen in a stagflationary trap (RBNZ). The NZ 2Y yield at 3.58% vs the JGB 2Y at 1.32% creates a +226bp front-end spread, but this is narrowing as JGB yields rise faster. The BoJ's March Summary of Opinions revealed debate on the size of the next hike, with at least one member suggesting a larger-than-25bp move — a 50bp hike to 1.25% is essentially unpriced and would compress the NZD-JPY differential by 50bp in a single meeting. The transmission mechanism is direct: each BoJ hike raises the cost of being long NZD/JPY via carry, reduces incentive for new positions, and triggers incremental covering of existing longs.
key_bilateral_metrics: NZD-JPY policy rate differential: +150bp (narrowing toward +125bp post-BoJ)
NZ 2Y–JGB 2Y spread: +226bp (narrowing)
NZ 10Y–JGB 10Y spread: +242bp (NZ 4.78% vs Japan 2.36%)
BoJ April hike probability: ~70% for +25bp; near-zero for +50bp (underpriced)
RBNZ April hold probability: ~100%
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has partially priced the April BoJ hike (~70%), meaning 30% of the move remains to be captured on delivery. The specific NZD/JPY carry compression is only partially reflected — the pair at 91.50 does not fully discount a +125bp carry environment.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 1–6 months, the BoJ is projected to deliver 1–2 additional hikes to 1.00–1.25%, compressing the NZD-JPY differential from +150bp toward +100–125bp. Meanwhile, RBNZ hikes are priced from September 2026 but uncertain — market pricing at ~2.50–2.75% end-2026 would only partially offset the BoJ's moves. The strategic carry trajectory therefore favours JPY: the BoJ is hiking with high conviction while the RBNZ's rate path is hostage to the oil shock's duration. If the oil shock proves temporary and the RBNZ hikes in September (+25bp to 2.50%), the carry differential stabilises near +125bp; if the BoJ reaches 1.25% first, it temporarily narrows to +100bp. The critical uncertainty is the BoJ's terminal rate — Goldman sees limited scope above 1.00%, while Tamura advocates 1.50–1.75%. Even the bearish-for-carry scenario of BoJ at 1.25% and RBNZ at 2.50% still leaves +125bp of positive carry for NZD/JPY — the carry doesn't disappear, it merely becomes less attractive.
key_bilateral_metrics: BoJ projected terminal rate: 1.00% (Goldman) to 1.75% (Tamura)
RBNZ projected end-2026: 2.25–2.75% (range of bank forecasts)
Carry differential trajectory: +150bp → +100–125bp by Q3 2026
Forward-implied NZD/JPY appreciation: ~1.5% annualised (from carry)
JGB 10Y at 2.36% — highest since 1999, still rising
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price a gradual carry compression consistent with BoJ normalisation. The underpriced scenario is a faster BoJ hike pace (50bp in April or consecutive hikes in April + June) that compresses carry below +100bp. The overpriced scenario is RBNZ hikes being pulled forward to Q2, which would widen carry.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons are bearish for NZD/JPY via the rates channel, but tactical confidence is higher (72 vs 58) because the April BoJ meeting is a specific, datable catalyst with high delivery probability, while the strategic path depends on multiple uncertain variables (BoJ terminal rate, RBNZ reaction, oil trajectory).

carry_analysis.historical_note: April 2025 drop from 87 to 79.81 (7.7 big figures) demonstrates carry provides negligible protection in risk-off unwind

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The NZ-Japan growth differential has narrowed sharply: NZ GDP at +0.2% QoQ / +1.3% YoY versus Japan at +0.3% QoQ / +1.3% YoY. However, the momentum divergence is stark — NZ business confidence collapsed from 59 to 33 in March while Japan's Reuters Tankan rose to +18 (strongest since December 2021). NZ's stagflationary profile (CPI 3.1% rising, growth stalling) paralyses the RBNZ, while Japan's inflation dynamics (headline CPI 1.3% but core-core at 2.5%, Shunto at 5.26%) validate the BoJ's hike path. The upcoming Q1 NZ CPI release (~April 16, expected 3.3–3.5%) will deepen the stagflation narrative. Japan's April Tankan is expected to confirm economic resilience. The bilateral transmission: worsening NZ data undermines NZD sentiment while strengthening Japan data reinforces BoJ hike expectations — both channels push NZD/JPY lower.
key_bilateral_metrics: NZ GDP: +0.2% QoQ vs Japan +0.3% QoQ — near convergence
NZ CPI: 3.1% vs Japan CPI: 1.3% (headline) — NZ 180bp higher but structurally different
NZ unemployment: 5.4% vs Japan: 2.6% — NZ 280bp higher, wide gap
ANZ Business Confidence: 32.5 vs Reuters Tankan: +18 — diverging sharply
Shunto: 5.26% (Japan wage growth validates BoJ normalisation)
tactical.what_is_priced: The stagflationary NZ data is partially priced in NZD weakness, but the upcoming Q1 CPI release could amplify if it exceeds 3.5%. Japan's data confirmation of economic resilience is only partially reflected in the 70% BoJ hike probability — a strong Tankan could push this above 80%.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the growth trajectories diverge if the Iran conflict persists: ASB projects NZ growth at just 1.3% with 'recovery unlikely until 2027,' while Japan's output gap is positive and the IMF endorses continued monetary tightening. However, both economies are vulnerable to the oil shock — Japan as a 90% energy importer and NZ as a 100% refined fuel importer. The key difference is that Japan can respond (BoJ hikes to fight imported inflation, maintaining currency credibility) while NZ cannot (RBNZ is stuck). If the conflict resolves, both economies recover, but NZ's recovery is slower given deeper structural damage (housing stagnation, migration slowdown, fiscal deterioration).
key_bilateral_metrics: NZ 2026 GDP forecast: 1.3% (ASB, post-conflict revision) vs Japan ~1.0–1.3%
NZ CPI peak forecast: ~4.0% mid-2026 vs Japan expected reacceleration to 2.5–3.0%
NZ current account: -3.5% of GDP vs Japan: +3.5% — 700bp chasm
Japan Shunto wage momentum: structural vs NZ Labour Cost Index: decelerating
strategic.what_is_priced: The bilateral growth differential is approximately correctly priced at current levels. The underpriced risk is a NZ recession scenario (Q2 GDP contraction) while Japan avoids it — this would push NZD/JPY sharply lower.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons bearish, with higher tactical confidence (68 vs 55) due to immediate data catalysts (NZ CPI, Japan Tankan) versus more uncertain strategic macro paths.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: NZD/JPY is a pure risk-proxy pair warranting +0.02 tactical uplift; strategic weight unchanged

tactical.bilateral_analysis: NZD/JPY is one of the purest risk-on/risk-off barometers in G10 FX, combining a high-beta commodity currency (NZD) with the deepest safe-haven currency (JPY). With VIX at 30.6, S&P 500 down 7.3% YTD, and the Iran conflict in its fifth week, the risk environment is firmly hostile for NZD/JPY longs. NZD/JPY is an excellent risk sentiment indicator — when global markets are optimistic, NZD/JPY tends to rise, and when markets are stressed, it falls. The standard correlation structure is partially intact: NZD is weakening as expected in risk-off, but JPY's safe-haven mechanism is partially suppressed by the energy import cost channel. This suppression limits NZD/JPY downside versus what a 'pure' risk-off would deliver — the pair has fallen from 94.86 to 91.50 (~3.5%) rather than the 10%+ that a typical VIX-30 environment would warrant. The dampened safe-haven mechanism is itself a risk: if the Iran conflict resolves, JPY safe-haven demand disappears while NZD rallies — a double upside risk for NZD/JPY. The Nikkei at -11.5% from highs is a relevant cross-asset signal for JPY flows.
key_bilateral_metrics: VIX: 30.6 (80th+ percentile, firmly risk-off)
NZD/JPY 52-week range percentile: 78th (elevated, vulnerable to risk-off drawdown)
S&P 500: -7.3% YTD; Nikkei: -11.5% from highs
AUD/JPY correlation: high (NZD/JPY tracks as secondary risk barometer)
Pair beta to VIX: estimated -0.4 to -0.6 (suppressed by energy channel)
tactical.what_is_priced: Current risk-off conditions are partially priced — the pair has fallen 3.5% from highs but not capitulated. The unpriced risk is further escalation (Hormuz deadline April 6) which could push VIX above 35 and NZD/JPY below 90. Conversely, a ceasefire is also unpriced and would trigger a sharp reversal above 93.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 1–6 months, the risk environment is genuinely binary. Conflict resolution would restore JPY's safe-haven channel (no longer suppressed) but also lift risk appetite globally — the net effect on NZD/JPY is ambiguous because NZD rallies harder in risk-on but JPY would initially strengthen on energy relief. Prolonged conflict deepens risk-off, benefiting JPY via the safe-haven channel if markets habituate to the energy cost. The neutral stance reflects genuine uncertainty about which scenario materialises.
key_bilateral_metrics: Historical post-conflict risk-on NZD/JPY rally: 5–10% over 3–6 months
Historical risk-off NZD/JPY drawdown: 10–15% in severe episodes (2008, Aug 2024)
JPY safe-haven beta currently suppressed — restoration would be JPY-positive
China GDP target: 4.5–5.0% — supports NZD via dairy demand
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price continued uncertainty. Any resolution clarity in either direction would move the pair significantly — the distribution is wide.

factors.horizon_split: Bearish tactically because risk-off is established and NZD/JPY's high beta amplifies. Neutral strategically because the 1–6 month outcome depends on geopolitical resolution — a binary variable.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The combined CFTC positioning picture for NZD/JPY is nuanced: NZD is at the 80th–90th percentile of net shorts (-41,742 contracts) while JPY is at the 32nd percentile (-62,806 contracts). For NZD/JPY, this means both legs are net short against USD — the implied pair positioning is moderately bearish NZD/JPY (specs are more extremely short NZD than JPY in relative terms). The NZD short is near exhaustion levels where contrarian signals activate, but the JPY short has room to cover on a BoJ hike, which would accelerate NZD/JPY downside. The key flow dynamic is Japanese retail investors (the 'Mrs. Watanabe' carry trade) who historically accumulate NZD/JPY as a carry vehicle — Japanese carry trade activity in the NZD has historically maintained a higher exchange rate for the Kiwi than would otherwise have been the case. A BoJ hike to 1.00% would raise the opportunity cost of this carry trade, potentially triggering retail long liquidation in NZD/JPY — a flow that doesn't show up in CFTC data.
key_bilateral_metrics: NZD CFTC net short: -41,742 (80th–90th percentile, extreme)
JPY CFTC net short: -62,806 (32nd percentile, moderate)
Net NZD/JPY implied: moderately bearish (NZD more crowded short)
Japanese retail NZD/JPY carry trade: structural but under pressure
Leveraged JPY funds covering +10,577 in latest week (reducing risk ahead of BoJ)
tactical.what_is_priced: The NZD bearish consensus is fully embedded. Incremental NZD-negative news has diminishing impact. A BoJ hike forcing JPY short covering would produce NZD/JPY downside even if NZD shorts are stable.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 1–6 months, the NZD extreme positioning (80th–90th percentile) should mean-revert toward neutral, providing some NZD/JPY upside support. Simultaneously, JPY shorts could build or cover depending on the BoJ's pace and the oil trajectory. The ~$500bn in JPY carry positions globally (Morgan Stanley estimate) includes NZD/JPY as a component — if carry-to-vol deteriorates as the BoJ hikes, institutional unwind could be a structural NZD/JPY headwind over months. The neutral stance reflects the offsetting forces.
key_bilateral_metrics: NZD positioning mean-reversion timeline: 8–16 weeks from extreme
JPY carry trade outstanding: ~$500bn globally
NZD/JPY carry-to-vol: ~0.13 (well below 0.40 accumulation threshold)
BoJ terminal rate trigger for carry unwind: ~1.25–1.50%
strategic.what_is_priced: Positioning is roughly in equilibrium for the pair. A carry trade unwind from JPY shorts is the underpriced tail risk.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons neutral — the pair-level positioning signal is washed out by opposing extremes on each leg.

tactical.combined_positioning_signal: Both legs net short against USD; NZD at 80th–90th percentile extreme, JPY at 32nd percentile moderate — implied net pair positioning moderately bearish NZD/JPY
tactical.unwind_trigger: A credible ceasefire or positive NZD data surprise (dairy surge, above-consensus CPI relief) could trigger NZD short-covering; BoJ hike triggers JPY short-covering which dominates
tactical.historical_analogue: August 2024 carry unwind: NZD/JPY dropped ~10 big figures in days when BoJ hiked unexpectedly, demonstrating the JPY short-covering channel overwhelms NZD dynamics
tactical.contrarian_case: The pair-specific contrarian signal is mixed. NZD's extreme short creates squeeze risk that could temporarily lift NZD/JPY on any positive NZD catalyst (ceasefire, dairy surge, positive data). However, the BoJ hike provides a specific JPY-bullish catalyst on April 27–28 that would overwhelm any NZD short-covering. The net contrarian assessment is neutral — opposing forces roughly balance and neither the long nor short NZD/JPY trade is obviously contrarian at this moment.

strategic.combined_positioning_signal: NZD extreme short will mean-revert over 8–16 weeks providing NZD/JPY upside; JPY shorts could build or cover depending on BoJ pace — net offsetting
strategic.unwind_trigger: BoJ normalisation above 1.25% triggers institutional carry unwind; NZD mean-reversion toward neutral positioning if data stabilises
strategic.historical_analogue: 2013 Abenomics JPY short build: NZD/JPY reached extremes as JPY weakened; eventual BoJ pivot forced large positioning adjustment across all JPY crosses
strategic.contrarian_case: NZD's extreme short at the 6-month horizon suggests eventual positioning-driven NZD recovery (bullish NZD/JPY). But BoJ normalisation simultaneously drains the JPY short side. These offsetting flows produce a net neutral positioning signal — neither a strong contrarian buy nor sell for NZD/JPY at the strategic horizon. The primary risk is the ~$500bn global JPY carry outstanding representing a structural unwind threat if carry-to-vol deteriorates sufficiently.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Both NZ and Japan are acutely vulnerable to the oil shock — NZ as a 100% refined fuel importer and Japan as a 90% energy importer. The net oil impact on NZD/JPY is roughly neutral because both economies suffer: NZ's terms of trade deteriorate from fuel costs while Japan's terms of trade deteriorate from crude/LNG/coal costs. However, the magnitude differs: Japan's import bill is vastly larger in absolute terms, and the ToT index at 91.51 faces steeper declines than NZ's. The dairy channel provides NZD-specific support: GDT prices up 18% YTD with WMP at US$3,709/MT and the April 7 auction approaching. NZ exports dairy, timber, aluminium, and beef to Japan — bilateral trade totalled US$6.4 billion, with NZ exporting timber, aluminium, dairy, kiwifruit, and beef to Japan while importing motor vehicles and electronic goods. NZ exports to Japan were US$2.32 billion during 2024. The current account chasm (NZ at -3.5% vs Japan at +3.5%) is a 700bp structural differential that would normally favour JPY strongly, but in practice Japan's primary income surplus doesn't generate JPY buying flow (reinvested offshore), dampening the FX transmission.
key_bilateral_metrics: Oil up: −NZD (100% fuel import) and −−JPY (90% energy import) — net: roughly neutral for the pair
Dairy up (+18% YTD): ++NZD, neutral JPY — net: bullish NZD/JPY
NZ current account: -3.5% vs Japan: +3.5% (700bp differential)
NZ exports to Japan: ~US$2.3bn annually
Brent: $107+/bbl — devastating for both
tactical.what_is_priced: The oil-negative channel is well-priced bilaterally. The dairy-positive channel is partially priced. The current account differential's structural drag on NZD is long-term and approximately correctly valued.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the external balance picture remains roughly balanced between NZD and JPY. If oil normalises to $85–95, both economies benefit, but Japan benefits proportionally more given its larger energy import bill — mildly NZD/JPY positive. If dairy prices sustain above $3,700/MT, NZ's structural export support provides a floor — also mildly NZD/JPY positive. The NZ current account improvement (-9.0% to -3.5%) represents structural de-risking that the market hasn't fully credited. The net commodity exposure calculation using direction rules: Oil ↑ = mildly −NZD and −−JPY → net mildly bullish NZD/JPY. Dairy ↑ = ++NZD and neutral JPY → bullish NZD/JPY. Combined with current account differential favouring JPY structurally, the net is approximately neutral.
key_bilateral_metrics: Oil normalisation scenario ($85–95): Japan benefits more → mildly NZD/JPY positive
Dairy sustained above $3,700: structural NZD support → NZD/JPY positive
NZ current account improvement: -9.0% → -3.5% (underappreciated)
Japan primary income: ¥41.59T (record) but limited FX impact
NZ NIIP: -47% GDP vs Japan NIIP: +¥533T — structural NZD drag
strategic.what_is_priced: The bilateral external balance is approximately fairly priced at current NZD/JPY levels. No clear directional edge.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons neutral — the oil shock hits both economies roughly equally in FX terms, while dairy provides NZD support and the current account gap provides JPY support, netting out.

net_commodity_exposure.base_commodity_profile: NZD: 100% refined fuel importer (oil negative), major dairy exporter (+18% YTD GDT prices, WMP at $3,709/MT), timber and aluminium exporter to Japan
net_commodity_exposure.quote_commodity_profile: JPY: 90% energy importer (crude, LNG, coal — highly oil negative), ToT index at 91.51 under severe pressure, no significant commodity export offsets
net_commodity_exposure.net_effect_calculation: Step 1 — Oil channel: NZD is 100% refined fuel importer (oil up = NZD negative, magnitude: moderate). JPY is 90% energy importer with much larger absolute import bill (oil up = JPY strongly negative, magnitude: large). Net effect of oil up: mildly bullish NZD/JPY because JPY is more negatively affected in absolute terms. Step 2 — Dairy channel: NZD is major dairy exporter (dairy up = NZD strongly positive). JPY has no dairy export offset (dairy up = neutral JPY). Net effect of dairy up: bullish NZD/JPY. Step 3 — Current account differential: NZ at -3.5% vs Japan at +3.5% is a 700bp structural gap normally favouring JPY, but Japan's primary income surplus is reinvested offshore limiting FX transmission. Step 4 — Combined: oil effect is mildly NZD/JPY positive, dairy effect is NZD/JPY positive, current account is structurally NZD/JPY negative. These forces approximately offset, yielding a net neutral signal.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_up_10pct: Net mildly bullish NZD/JPY: Japan's larger energy import bill suffers proportionally more, partially offsetting NZD's 100% fuel import burden. The pair may see 50–100 pip NZD/JPY upside in isolation, but risk-off from oil spike likely dominates with a net negative pair result.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_down_10pct: Net mildly bearish NZD/JPY: Japan benefits proportionally more from energy cost relief, improving JPY terms of trade. Risk-on from oil decline lifts NZD but JPY energy relief provides greater fundamental support — estimated 50–80 pip NZD/JPY downside from the ToT channel, offset partially by risk appetite recovery.
net_commodity_exposure.dominant_commodity_driver: Dairy (GDT WMP) is the dominant pair-specific commodity driver because it is exclusively NZD-positive with no JPY offset — dairy price changes create clean directional NZD/JPY moves. Oil is a shared headwind that partially nets out, making dairy the differentiating commodity variable for this specific cross.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The guidance divergence between the BoJ and RBNZ is the widest in years. The BoJ has never been more hawkish: the Summary of Opinions (March 30) includes explicit calls to 'raise without hesitation,' Takata dissented for a hike at the March meeting, and Tamura advocates a 1.50–1.75% neutral rate. By contrast, RBNZ Governor Breman's March 23 speech explicitly stated the bank would 'look through' the oil shock — providing no carry improvement signal. The April 8 RBNZ MPR (no new forecasts, expected hold) falls two days after the Hormuz deadline, but carries little information content. The April 27–28 BoJ MPM with Outlook Report is the dominant guidance event. The bilateral transmission is asymmetric: the BoJ signals more tightening (JPY-positive) while the RBNZ signals continued accommodation (NZD-neutral to negative). Both channels push NZD/JPY lower.
key_bilateral_metrics: BoJ guidance: 'raise without hesitation' (Summary of Opinions)
RBNZ guidance: 'look through' temporary oil disruption
BoJ credibility: delivered 3 hikes as signalled (high trust)
RBNZ credibility: untested under Breman, market prices more hawkish than track
Gap: BoJ tightening actively vs RBNZ frozen
tactical.what_is_priced: The BoJ's hawkish bias is partially priced (~70% April hike). The RBNZ's dovish stance is fully priced. The bilateral guidance divergence is the key driver — any further hawkish BoJ shift (larger hike, accelerated pace) is unpriced.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The May 27 RBNZ MPS is the pivotal domestic NZD event — it will reveal whether the RBNZ acknowledges inflation deanchoring and lifts the OCR track, or maintains 'look through' guidance. The June and July BoJ meetings provide additional normalisation opportunities. Over 6 months, the BoJ is expected to build credibility through continued delivery while the RBNZ faces a credibility test (consumer 2Y inflation expectations at 5.7% are alarming). The guidance differential favours JPY but with diminishing certainty at longer horizons.
key_bilateral_metrics: May 27: RBNZ MPS (new OCR track — critical for NZD)
June 15: BoJ MPM + QT interim assessment
July 30: BoJ MPM + Outlook Report (potential second hike)
NZ consumer 2Y inflation expectations: 5.7% (credibility risk)
BoJ terminal rate debate: 1.00–1.75% (wide range)
strategic.what_is_priced: The strategic guidance trajectory is moderately priced. The underpriced scenario is a hawkish RBNZ pivot at May MPS (NZD/JPY positive). The overpriced scenario is the market's assumption that the BoJ stops at 1.00% — Tamura's advocacy for 1.50%+ suggests otherwise.

factors.horizon_split: Both bearish; tactical confidence higher (68 vs 55) due to the April BoJ meeting's immediacy.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The event calendar is exceptionally dense and skewed bearish for NZD/JPY. The April 6 Hormuz deadline creates binary risk: escalation is NZD/JPY negative (oil spike + risk-off), while de-escalation is ambiguous (NZD rallies on risk-on but JPY rallies on energy relief — partially offsetting). The April 7 GDT auction provides a micro-catalyst (positive dairy = NZD support). The April 8 RBNZ MPR is low-information. The Q1 NZ CPI (~April 16, expected 3.3–3.5%) deepens stagflation if above consensus. The April 27–28 BoJ meeting is the dominant event — a hike is NZD/JPY bearish via carry compression. This immediately precedes Golden Week (April 29–May 5), creating a liquidity vacuum that amplifies the BoJ impact. The event clustering creates compounding asymmetry: the BoJ hike + Golden Week thin markets + potential Hormuz escalation could produce outsized NZD/JPY downside.
key_bilateral_metrics: April 6: Hormuz deadline (binary)
April 7: GDT Auction (mildly bullish NZD/JPY if positive)
April 8: RBNZ MPR (neutral, no new info)
~April 16: NZ Q1 CPI (bearish NZD/JPY if 3.5%+)
April 27–28: BoJ MPM (bearish NZD/JPY on hike delivery)
tactical.what_is_priced: The BoJ hike is ~70% priced; the remaining 30% provides downside on delivery. The Golden Week amplification is not priced. The Hormuz deadline is not priced in either direction.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 1–6 months, the event calendar is loaded on both sides. The May 27–28 NZ cluster (RBNZ MPS + Budget) could be NZD-supportive or NZD-negative depending on the RBNZ's response. The BoJ has three additional meetings (June, July, September). The NZ November 7 election introduces political risk. Events are symmetric over the longer horizon — multiple potential positive and negative outcomes for NZD/JPY balance roughly.
key_bilateral_metrics: May 27–28: RBNZ MPS + NZ Budget (NZD-variable)
June 15: BoJ MPM + QT assessment (JPY-positive if hawkish)
July 30: BoJ MPM + Outlook Report (potential second hike)
November 7: NZ General Election (NZD risk premium)
strategic.what_is_priced: Strategic events are generally unpriced because outcomes are unknown. The NZ election adds a late-year risk premium not currently in NZD/JPY.

factors.horizon_split: Bearish tactically (55) due to the concentration of negative catalysts in April. Neutral strategically (45) as events are more balanced over 6 months.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral fiscal picture is mixed but not acutely material over 2–4 weeks. NZ's Fitch outlook downgrade to Negative (AA+) contrasts with Japan's S&P A+ stable (with a warning about yen weakness). NZ debt at ~50% of GDP is high for its rating but manageable; Japan at ~236% is the highest in the developed world but tolerated due to domestic ownership. Neither country faces an imminent fiscal crisis. The S&P warning that Japan's rating could be lowered if the yen weakens 'much further' creates a negative feedback loop that could become NZD/JPY-relevant if USD/JPY pushes past 162. The Fitch NZ downgrade adds a marginal NZD risk premium. Net effect: approximately neutral for the pair tactically.
key_bilateral_metrics: NZ: Fitch AA+ Negative; Moody's Aaa Stable; S&P AA+ Stable
Japan: S&P A+ Stable (with yen-weakness warning)
NZ gross debt: ~50% of GDP vs Japan: ~236% of GDP
NZ NZGB 10Y at 4.78% (includes fiscal premium)
JGB 30Y at 3.71% (+117bp YoY — fiscal/term premium rising)
tactical.what_is_priced: The Fitch NZ downgrade is absorbed. Japan's fiscal risk is structurally priced as a non-issue. Neither fiscal position is creating a pair-level trade.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, both countries face fiscal deterioration — NZ from oil-shock revenue shortfalls and pre-election spending, Japan from record ¥122T budget and rising JGB servicing costs. The Budget 2026 (May 28) is the key NZ event. The fiscal trajectories are both negative but for different reasons, and neither dominates the pair at this horizon. Japan's fiscal risk is a slow-burning JPY headwind while NZ's is a discrete event risk. Net: neutral.
key_bilateral_metrics: NZ Budget 2026: May 28 (pre-election fiscal statement)
Japan FY2026 budget: ¥122T+ (record)
NZ return to surplus: not until FY2029/30
Japan interest bill: projected to double by 2031 (IMF)
strategic.what_is_priced: Fiscal concerns are background noise for NZD/JPY. No clear pair-level edge.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons neutral — fiscal dynamics are approximately equally negative for both currencies, netting out at the pair level.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: NZD/JPY at 91.50 trades below the 50-day SMA (92.72) and 100-day SMA (92.30) but above the 200-day SMA (90.00). The MA configuration is partially bearish: spot below the shorter MAs with the 50-day angling lower confirms near-term momentum is negative, but the 200-day provides a clear support floor 150 pips below. Technical analysis suggests selling rallies around 92.95–93.00 with stops near 94.18, targeting 90.00, with the 94.20 resistance as the key barrier. NZD/JPY appears exhausted from its climb, with Middle East tensions and recession fears limiting upside while JPY benefits from risk-off flows as the Strait of Hormuz stays closed. The estimated pair IV of 11–13% at a modest percentile (50th–60th range) suggests options are not excessively expensive, supporting options-based expressions. RV at ~8–9% is below IV, confirming a small volatility risk premium — event premium is embedded but not yet realised in spot.
key_bilateral_metrics: 52-week range: 79.81–94.86 (78th percentile)
Support levels: 91.00, 90.50/90.00 (200d SMA confluence), 89.00
Resistance levels: 92.50, 93.00, 94.20
Estimated 1M IV: ~11–13%; RV: ~8–9% (IV > RV)
tactical.what_is_priced: The near-term downtrend is established in systematic models. The 200-day SMA at 90.00 is the key level — a break below would trigger further systematic selling and signal a trend regime change.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 1–6 months, the pair's range is wide enough (79.81–94.86) to accommodate significant moves in either direction. The 200-day SMA at 90.00 is rising and could provide dynamic support as the pair consolidates. Historical NZD/JPY behaviour shows the pair typically ranges 10–15 big figures over 6 months, suggesting a potential range of 84–97 from current levels depending on geopolitical resolution and BoJ path. The strategic technical picture is genuinely neutral — trend momentum favours downside but valuation and positioning favour eventual recovery.
key_bilateral_metrics: 200d SMA rising: currently 90.00, trending toward 90.50–91.00 over 3 months
6-month typical NZD/JPY range: 10–15 big figures
Downside technical target (descending triangle breakdown): ~88–89
Upside technical target (triangle breakout + dairy rally): ~95–96
strategic.what_is_priced: The strategic technical landscape is broadly neutral. Neither a breakout nor breakdown is clearly favoured by the structure alone.

factors.horizon_split: Bearish tactically (60) on the descending triangle and below-MA configuration; neutral strategically (48) as the wide 52-week range and rising 200-day create a more balanced longer-term picture.

expression_assessment.vol_landscape_summary: NZD/JPY estimated 1M IV at 11–13% sits at the 50th–60th percentile of the recent range, with RV at ~8–9% — a modest volatility risk premium exists. Event premium is embedded for the April 6 Hormuz deadline and April 27–28 BoJ meeting, with vol term structure likely in backwardation around these dates.
expression_assessment.directional_expression: The bearish near-term view is best expressed through spot short NZD/JPY with a stop above 93.00 (above descending triangle resistance), targeting 90.00 initially and 88.50 on follow-through. Options are moderately priced (IV slightly above RV), making NZD/JPY put spreads a capital-efficient alternative — a 1M 91.00/89.00 put spread would capture the BoJ hike event with defined risk.
expression_assessment.event_timing_consideration: For event-specific positioning around April 27–28, short-dated (1-week) options would be efficient given the likely vol spike. Post-BoJ hike delivery, extending the position into Golden Week thin markets (April 29–May 5) captures the amplification effect in illiquid conditions.
expression_assessment.liquidity_note: NZD/JPY is a liquid G10 cross traded through the NZD/USD × USD/JPY vehicle currency channel. Execution is straightforward during Tokyo and London sessions; Golden Week (April 29–May 5) creates a structural liquidity vacuum that amplifies moves but also creates slippage risk — position sizing should be reduced during this window.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: Non-USD cross pair where USD funding is less directly impactful; reduced by 0.02 at each horizon

tactical.bilateral_analysis: NZD/JPY is a non-USD cross, but both currencies are traded primarily through the vehicle currency channel (NZD/USD × USD/JPY). The USD funding environment affects NZD/JPY through this channel: tighter USD conditions raise the cost of funding both positions. DXY at ~100.3 with safe-haven demand active creates modest headwinds for both NZD and JPY (both sell against USD in risk-off), with the net NZD/JPY impact negligible. The critical bilateral funding note is that the RBNZ does NOT have a Fed swap line while the BoJ does — in a USD liquidity crisis, NZ institutions face higher funding costs than Japanese institutions, which is structurally NZD/JPY negative during stress. However, no acute USD funding stress is present (SOFR stable, BoJ swap line usage near zero).
key_bilateral_metrics: DXY: ~100.3 (neutral-to-modestly tight)
SOFR: ~3.55–3.60% (stable)
RBNZ Fed swap line: NONE (vulnerability)
BoJ USD swap line: $30.3M outstanding (no stress)
Dollar smile: left side (risk-off) — affects both legs equally
tactical.what_is_priced: USD liquidity conditions are approximately neutral for NZD/JPY. The swap-line asymmetry is a tail risk not currently active.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 1–6 months, USD liquidity evolves with the Fed's path. QT has ended (neutral-to-positive), and the Fed projects one cut in 2026. If the Fed delivers a cut, both NZD/USD and USD/JPY would react — NZD/USD would rise (NZD-positive) while USD/JPY would fall (JPY-positive). The net NZD/JPY effect is ambiguous. The Warsh Fed Chair transition (May 15) introduces uncertainty. Rising JGB yields (10Y at 2.36%) make domestic Japanese bonds more attractive, potentially reducing offshore allocation and USD borrowing — a slow JPY-positive structural force. Net: neutral for the cross pair.
key_bilateral_metrics: Fed projected path: 1 cut in 2026 (market: hold)
QT ended Dec 2025 (neutral-to-positive)
JGB 10Y at 2.36% — increasing domestic appeal
Warsh transition: May 15 (communication uncertainty)
strategic.what_is_priced: USD liquidity is approximately correctly priced for NZD/JPY. No bilateral edge.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons neutral — USD funding conditions affect both legs similarly, with no clear pair-level directional signal.

adjustments.reason: NZD/JPY is a pure risk-proxy pair combining high-beta commodity currency (NZD) with deep safe-haven (JPY); risk sentiment has outsized relevance warranting +0.02 tactical uplift
Non-USD cross pair — both legs affected similarly by USD conditions, reducing direct USD liquidity relevance; -0.02 at tactical horizon

tactical.score_interpretation: Net score of -0.479 maps to Conviction 3/5 — moderately bearish NZD/JPY. The score is pulled lower by the three highest-weighted factors (Policy -0.173, Inflation -0.122, Risk -0.078) which together contribute 78% of the total bearish signal.
tactical.rationale: The tactical bearish NZD/JPY thesis rests on the convergence of the BoJ's imminent rate hike (Factor 1: -0.173, dominant contributor) and NZ's deteriorating stagflationary backdrop (Factor 2: -0.122). The BoJ is ~70% likely to deliver a 25bp hike to 1.00% on April 27–28, compressing the NZD-JPY carry differential from +150bp to +125bp — each basis point of compression reduces the incentive to hold NZD/JPY longs. The NZD leg is independently weak: the RBNZ is frozen at 2.25% with 'look through' guidance, NZ business confidence has collapsed (ANZ 59→33), and the Q1 CPI release (~April 16) will likely show further acceleration toward 3.5%. Risk appetite (Factor 3: -0.078) reinforces — VIX at 30.6 and NZD/JPY's high beta to risk make the pair vulnerable to any further escalation. The dossier stances are directionally aligned: the NZD dossier is bearish NZD while the JPY dossier is bullish JPY — both push NZD/JPY lower, creating a reinforcing pair signal rather than a conflict. The April 6 Hormuz deadline and April 27–28 BoJ meeting create a concentrated event cluster where downside risks dominate: escalation + hike = NZD/JPY to 88–89; de-escalation alone doesn't clearly lift NZD/JPY because JPY would also rally on energy relief. Conviction is limited to 3/5 rather than 4/5 because three factors contribute zero (Positioning, External Balance, USD Liquidity), and the extreme NZD short positioning creates squeeze risk on any positive NZD catalyst. The carry-to-vol ratio of only 0.13 confirms the pair offers insufficient compensation for vol risk, reducing the pool of carry accumulators who would resist a downside move. The event clustering around April 27–28 BoJ and Golden Week creates a specific time window where the bearish thesis is most likely to materialise with amplified magnitude.
tactical.g10_relative_ranking: NZD/JPY ranks as the 2nd most attractive short among JPY crosses (behind AUD/JPY which has a wider carry differential at 335bp and higher beta). Among NZD crosses, being short NZD/JPY is less attractive than short NZD/USD (cleaner expression of NZD weakness) but more attractive than short NZD/CHF (lower liquidity). Overall, NZD/JPY is a high-conviction pair trade because both dossiers align directionally — NZD bearish and JPY bullish produce a clean pair signal with no cross-directional noise.

conditions: Iran conflict persists without major escalation
RBNZ holds with dovish tone at April MPR
base_case.path: Sideways grind at 91–92 through mid-April, step-down to 90–91 on BoJ hike delivery, Golden Week thin markets amplify decline to 89.50

conditions: Dairy auction surges above $4,000/MT triggering NZD short squeeze of 200+ pips
NZ CPI above 3.5% confirming stagflation trap with risk-off deepening VIX above 35

tactical.path_expectation: NZD/JPY is expected to trade sideways-to-lower through mid-April (91–92 range), with downside bias accelerating as the BoJ meeting approaches (April 20+). The pair should break below 91.00 around the BoJ meeting date as pre-positioning ahead of the hike intensifies. On delivery, expect a 100–150 pip decline within 24 hours (from ~91 to 89.50–90.00), amplified by Golden Week liquidity vacuum (April 29–May 5). Intraday rallies toward 92.50 in the pre-meeting period represent selling opportunities. The April 6 Hormuz deadline creates a volatility spike that could temporarily push the pair in either direction by 100–200 pips before the broader trend reasserts. The path is therefore non-linear: a pre-Hormuz consolidation phase, a potential whipsaw around April 6, a renewed grind lower into the BoJ meeting, and then a step-down acceleration post-delivery amplified by Golden Week thin conditions.

increase_conditions: BoJ Summary of Opinions confirms 50bp hike debate AND VIX exceeds 35 AND NZ Q1 CPI >= 3.5% → increase to 4/5
BoJ hikes 50bp to 1.25% AND Hormuz escalates to full closure AND NZ enters recession → increase to 5/5
decrease_conditions: Ceasefire talks show material progress AND oil drops below $95 AND NZ data stabilises (ANZ BO >= 45) → decrease to 2/5
Credible ceasefire signed AND BoJ holds with dovish guidance AND dairy exceeds $4,000/MT → decrease to 1/5

risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Positively skewed for the bearish thesis. The downside case (30% probability, 350 pips) contributes more expected value than the upside risk (20% probability, -200 pips). The BoJ hike is a near-certain event while the upside requires multiple catalysts (ceasefire + hold + data).

strategic.score_interpretation: Net score of -0.260 maps to Conviction 2/5 — weakly bearish NZD/JPY. The score is driven almost entirely by three bearish factors (Policy, Inflation, Guidance: total -0.260) with seven factors contributing zero.
strategic.rationale: The strategic bearish NZD/JPY thesis is narrower and less confident than the tactical case. It rests on the BoJ delivering 1–2 additional hikes to 1.00–1.25% over 6 months, compressing carry from +150bp toward +100–125bp, while the RBNZ remains uncertain. Factor 1 (Policy) dominates at -0.139 but with lower confidence (58%) reflecting uncertainty about the BoJ's terminal rate and whether RBNZ hikes offset BoJ moves. The critical difference from the tactical analysis is that seven of ten factors shift to neutral strategically: risk appetite is a coin-flip depending on conflict resolution; external balance nets out bilaterally; positioning is offsetting; fiscal risk is equally negative for both; market structure is neutral; and USD liquidity is a wash for this cross. The strategic view is therefore a concentrated bet on the rate differential narrowing thesis — if the BoJ stops at 1.00% and the RBNZ starts hiking from September, the carry advantage could stabilise or even widen, invalidating the bearish thesis. The pair's 14% NZD undervaluation vs JPY on PPP provides a medium-term floor, limiting further downside. The risk/reward skew is less attractive strategically because the pair at 91.50 is not obviously expensive — it's roughly mid-range on a 6-month view. The primary edge is that the BoJ's normalisation cycle is a structural process that operates across multiple meetings, while NZD's structural disadvantages (stagflation, no swap line, fiscal deterioration) persist. The May 27–28 NZ cluster (RBNZ MPS + Budget) is the key inflection point: a hawkish RBNZ pivot would narrow the guidance gap and lift strategic conviction toward neutral, while a frozen RBNZ increases it toward 3/5. The staircase pattern of BoJ hikes (April, potentially July) provides a structural backdrop for stepped NZD/JPY declines, moderated by 200–300 pip countertrend rallies between meetings as carry accumulation and NZD short-covering provide intermittent support.
strategic.g10_relative_ranking: Strategically, NZD/JPY ranks 3rd among JPY shorts (behind AUD/JPY and GBP/JPY which have wider carry differentials and stronger rate divergence). Among NZD shorts, it ranks behind AUD/NZD long (cleaner Antipodean divergence) and NZD/USD short (more liquid, avoids JPY complexity). The pair's strategic ranking is weaker than tactical because the BoJ-specific catalyst fades once delivered, and the remaining bearish thesis is more diffuse.

conditions: BoJ hikes to 1.00% in April and possibly 1.25% in July
Iran conflict partially de-escalates with oil settling to $90–100/bbl
RBNZ lifts OCR track modestly at May MPS but holds through H1
Dairy sustains above $3,500/MT providing NZD floor
base_case.path: Decline through April–May (BoJ hikes), stabilisation in June–July (conflict resolution offsets BoJ), further decline in August–September if BoJ hikes again. Staircase pattern with 200–300 pip countertrend rallies between meetings.

conditions: Quick conflict resolution with oil below $85/bbl and BoJ pauses at 1.00% citing uncertainty
RBNZ hikes in September (+25bp to 2.50%) with dairy above $4,000 triggering NZD short squeeze
Prolonged conflict with oil sustained above $110/bbl and BoJ hikes to 1.25%+
NZ recession confirmed with RBNZ unable to respond and carry trade unwind accelerating

strategic.path_expectation: NZD/JPY is expected to follow a stepped decline over 6 months, with each BoJ meeting (April, June, July) acting as a discrete step-down catalyst. Phase 1 (April–May): BoJ hike to 1.00% pushes pair to 89–90, amplified by Golden Week thin markets. Phase 2 (June–July): partial conflict resolution provides a temporary bounce to 90–91, but June QT assessment and July meeting maintain downward pressure if a second hike materialises. Phase 3 (August–September): if BoJ reaches 1.25% and the NZD remains weak, the pair approaches 87–88. Expect 200–300 pip countertrend rallies between BoJ meetings as carry accumulation and NZD short-covering provide intermittent support. The shape is a staircase down with flat consolidation phases between meeting-driven steps. The May 27–28 NZ cluster is the key inflection point that determines whether the staircase continues or flattens — a hawkish RBNZ MPS would interrupt the decline and trigger a 150–250 pip bounce.

increase_conditions: BoJ delivers April hike AND oil stays above $100/bbl AND NZ Q1 GDP stagnates → increase to 3/5
BoJ reaches 1.25% AND Iran conflict persists past June AND NZ enters recession → increase to 4/5
decrease_conditions: BoJ pauses at 1.00% with dovish guidance AND conflict resolves AND RBNZ hikes to 2.50% → decrease to 1/5
NZD/JPY reclaims 93.00 on sustained basis AND carry-to-vol ratio rises above 0.30 → decrease to 1/5

risk_reward.expected_value: (0.50 × +200) + (0.25 × +600) + (0.25 × -300) = 100 + 150 - 75 = +175 pips gross; net ~+115 pips after carry cost (~60 pips over 6 months at +125–150bp annualised)
risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Moderately positive on a probability-weighted basis. The downside tail is larger than the upside tail, but carry cost at +125–150bp annualised erodes ~60 pips over 6 months, reducing net expected value to ~+115 pips. The strategic edge is thinner than the tactical case.

consensus_divergence.bank_consensus_forecast: Algorithmic forecast models project NZD/JPY to range between ¥93.78 and ¥107.92 in 2026 with an average around ¥100.94; one model projects a December 2026 range of 92.85–96.65 with average near 94.75. MUFG's pre-conflict forecast implied NZD/JPY in the 90–96 range.
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: 300–500 pips below consensus on a 6-month view
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: We differ from consensus on three key dimensions. First, consensus models are predominantly pre-conflict and have not incorporated the Iran oil shock's asymmetric impact on the NZD-JPY rate differential dynamic — the shock is simultaneously weakening both currencies but creating a specific BoJ hawkish catalyst that consensus underweights. Second, we assign higher probability to the BoJ exceeding 1.00% than consensus — the Summary of Opinions debate on hike size and Tamura's 1.50–1.75% advocacy are being underweighted by algorithmic models that extrapolate from gradualist BoJ history. Third, NZ's stagflationary trap is deeper than consensus assumes — ASB's 1.3% growth forecast with 'recovery unlikely until 2027' is not yet reflected in bank NZD/JPY forecasts, which largely predate the Iran conflict's economic transmission to NZ.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: 300–500 pips of alpha on a short NZD/JPY position over 6 months if NZD/JPY underperforms consensus by 3–5 big figures
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: If the conflict resolves quickly and the BoJ pauses, the pair recovers toward 93–95 — an opportunity cost of the missed 150–350 pip rally from current levels

cross_horizon_consistency.explanation: Tactical and strategic stances are aligned — both bearish NZD/JPY. Conviction differs (3/5 tactical vs 2/5 strategic) because the tactical case has a specific datable catalyst (BoJ April hike) while the strategic case depends on the uncertain continuation of the normalisation cycle, potential RBNZ response, and geopolitical resolution. The lower strategic conviction reflects that seven of ten factors are neutral at the longer horizon, leaving the bear thesis concentrated in the rates/guidance channel.
cross_horizon_consistency.inflection_point: May 27–28 NZ cluster (RBNZ MPS + Budget): if RBNZ lifts OCR track materially and the Budget is credible, strategic conviction could decrease toward 1/5; if May MPS reveals RBNZ still frozen, strategic conviction increases to 3/5
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Establish tactical short NZD/JPY into the BoJ meeting, then reassess after the May NZ cluster to determine whether to extend or cover the strategic leg of the position.

forward_vs_spot.forward_1m: ~91.61 (+11 pips from carry differential; NZD at slight premium)
forward_vs_spot.edge_3m: +232 pips (forward implies 91.82; we expect 89.50 — short NZD/JPY earns this gap)
forward_vs_spot.edge_6m: +305 pips (forward implies 92.05; we expect 89.00 — short NZD/JPY earns this gap)

catalysts.rationale: De-escalation triggers NZD rally (risk-on) but also JPY rally (energy relief) — net NZD/JPY impact is partially offsetting. Escalation pushes oil higher, hurting both economies but JPY more via energy import costs — mildly NZD/JPY positive from the ToT channel paradoxically, but risk-off flow and safe-haven demand for JPY could overwhelm this, pushing NZD/JPY sharply lower. The binary nature of this event creates a vol spike regardless of direction.

Dairy prices are up 18% YTD with WMP at US$3,709/MT. Another positive auction — particularly a print above $3,800–4,000/MT — would reinforce NZD's structural commodity floor and trigger incremental NZD buying from positioning-light accounts. A sharp decline of 5%+ in WMP would compound NZD weakness and could accelerate the NZD/JPY downtrend. The dairy channel is purely NZD-specific with no JPY offset, making this a clean directional NZD/JPY catalyst.

Unanimous hold at 2.25% is fully expected with no new forecasts released at this review. The statement language is the only signal — any deviation from Governor Breman's 'look through' stance would reprice hike expectations and be NZD/JPY positive. Maintenance of the existing stance is fully priced and provides no new information. The timing two days after the Hormuz deadline adds contextual uncertainty to the statement's tone.

Consensus expects 3.3–3.5% YoY. A print above 3.5% deepens the stagflation narrative, confirms RBNZ paralysis, and adds downward pressure on NZD as the market prices a longer hold at 2.25%. A below-consensus print (below 3.0%, highly unlikely given oil shock) would provide significant NZD relief. The bilateral transmission is asymmetric: high NZ CPI is NZD-negative (stagflation) while Japan's inflation data validates BoJ hikes (JPY-positive). Both channels push NZD/JPY lower on a hot NZ CPI print.

~70% probability of +25bp hike to 1.00%. The updated Outlook Report will include revised CPI forecasts higher given the oil shock, reinforcing the hawkish trajectory. Delivery compresses NZD-JPY carry from +150bp to +125bp and triggers JPY short-covering across all crosses including NZD/JPY. The unpriced scenario — a 50bp hike to 1.25% — would be massively JPY-positive and push NZD/JPY toward 88–89 in a single session. The 30% probability of a hold would trigger sharp NZD/JPY reversal toward 93.

catalysts.event: Golden Week begins (Japan market holidays through May 5)
catalysts.rationale: Japanese market holidays create thin liquidity, amplifying any moves from the preceding BoJ decision and upcoming FOMC meeting. Historically associated with sharp JPY moves in either direction during thin market conditions. If the BoJ has just delivered a hike, the Golden Week vacuum amplifies the bearish NZD/JPY momentum with limited two-way flow to absorb selling pressure. This is a structural amplifier for the BoJ catalyst, not an independent directional signal.

The first RBNZ forecast update fully incorporating the oil shock. The new OCR track is the key signal — a higher track (lifting hike expectations to Q4 2026) would be NZD-positive and NZD/JPY supportive, potentially triggering a 150–250 pip countertrend rally. An unchanged track (maintaining 'look through') would be NZD-negative and confirm the strategic bearish case. Consumer 2Y inflation expectations at 5.7% create credibility pressure on the RBNZ to act, making a hawkish surprise more possible than consensus assumes.

Pre-election budget balancing fiscal credibility with spending commitments. Wider deficits relative to BEFU projections would be NZD-negative, raising sovereign risk premium and signalling fiscal deterioration. A credible consolidation plan would be NZD-positive but politically risky in a pre-election context. Given NZ's Fitch Negative outlook and existing fiscal pressures from the oil shock, the risk is skewed toward disappointment — making the most likely outcome modestly NZD-negative.

catalysts.event: BoJ MPM + Outlook Report (potential second hike)
catalysts.rationale: Potential second hike to 1.25% with updated FY2027 forecasts. ~40–50% cumulative probability for two hikes by July based on current BoJ trajectory. A second hike would compress NZD-JPY carry to +100bp, approaching the level where the pair's carry-to-vol ratio becomes negative in elevated vol environments. The updated Outlook Report with FY2027 forecasts would signal whether the BoJ sees a path to 1.50%+, which is the key strategic catalyst for accelerated NZD/JPY downside.

A knife-edge electoral race with significant policy divergence between main parties. Centre-right victory (National-led) represents policy continuity and is NZD-neutral to mildly positive. Labour-Green coalition would introduce CGT uncertainty and spending increases that are initially NZD-negative. NZ First as potential kingmaker adds significant unpredictability to fiscal and monetary policy settings. The election introduces a late-year NZD risk premium that is not currently priced into NZD/JPY, creating an option-value argument for maintaining NZD/JPY exposure through year-end.

invalidators.condition: NZD/JPY closes above 94.20 (descending triangle resistance) for 3 consecutive sessions
invalidators.mechanism: The bearish technical structure would be comprehensively broken, the near-term downtrend invalidated, and momentum would shift to the NZD-bullish side. A sustained break above 94.20 implies the dairy + ceasefire + BoJ pause scenario is materialising simultaneously, removing the primary bearish catalysts.

invalidators.condition: BoJ holds in April 27–28 meeting with dovish statement and removes tightening bias language
invalidators.mechanism: The primary tactical catalyst would be removed entirely. The carry differential would remain at +150bp with no near-term compression signal, and carry trade accumulation in NZD/JPY would resume. The BoJ hold would also signal that risk-off/uncertainty concerns are overwhelming the inflation mandate, potentially triggering a sharp NZD/JPY reversal toward 93.50+.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: BoJ April 27–28 statement and press conference language

invalidators.condition: Brent crude falls below $85/bbl sustained for 2+ weeks
invalidators.mechanism: Both the NZD and JPY headwinds from the oil shock would be substantially defused. Japan's terms-of-trade improvement from energy relief would be proportionally larger than NZ's, providing greater JPY fundamental support — the net effect on NZD/JPY is ambiguous but removes the shared-pain dynamic that has suppressed both currencies. Risk appetite would also recover, potentially lifting NZD more than JPY in risk-on.

invalidators.condition: RBNZ surprises with a rate hike at the April 8 MPR or May 27 MPS (25bp to 2.50%)
invalidators.mechanism: A 25bp RBNZ hike would widen the NZD-JPY carry to +175bp (or maintain at +150bp post-BoJ hike), trigger a significant NZD short squeeze from the 80th–90th percentile extreme positioning, signal RBNZ credibility on inflation control, and eliminate the 'RBNZ frozen' narrative that is the NZD-negative leg of the pair thesis. Combined with any positive risk backdrop, NZD/JPY would surge 200–350 pips.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: RBNZ OCR announcement and OCR track projections

invalidators.condition: Japan's monthly current account flips to deficit on an MoF release
invalidators.mechanism: The fundamental case for JPY strength would be severely undermined, signalling that the energy shock has overwhelmed Japan's primary income buffer. A current account deficit would eliminate the structural JPY support from trade surplus dynamics and would likely trigger rating agency commentary on Japan's external position — a rare and powerful bearish JPY catalyst that would overwhelm the BoJ hike story and push NZD/JPY significantly higher.

invalidators.condition: NZD/JPY breaks below the 200-day SMA (90.00) and sustains for 3+ sessions
invalidators.mechanism: The break of the key structural support would confirm the bearish thesis comprehensively, trigger systematic model selling across CTAs and quant funds, remove the 200-day as a reference for stop placement, and signal a potential acceleration toward the 88–89 technical target. This is an invalidator of the existing conviction level but a confirmation of directional stance — it changes conviction rather than direction.

signposts.confirms_if: Stays above $100/bbl — energy shock persists, maintaining both currency headwinds with rate differential as dominant pair driver
signposts.invalidates_if: Falls below $85/bbl — risk relief disproportionately helps JPY via ToT improvement, creating offsetting forces
signposts.bilateral_importance: The oil price determines which currency suffers more in the bilateral framework. Above $100, the pair thesis is driven by rate differentials (BoJ vs RBNZ); below $90, terms-of-trade relief helps JPY more than NZD because Japan's energy import bill is proportionally larger, creating potential NZD/JPY upside even as both currencies recover. This makes oil the single most important macro variable for the NZD/JPY thesis.

signposts.confirms_if: Spot breaks below 90.00 and sustains for 3+ sessions — bearish trend confirmation and systematic selling trigger
signposts.invalidates_if: Spot bounces from 90.00 and reclaims the 50-day SMA (92.72) — trend reversal signal
signposts.bilateral_importance: The 200d SMA is the key structural support specific to this pair, providing the reference level for stop placement across systematic and discretionary traders. A breach would trigger CTA model re-positioning across NZD/JPY specifically, given its role as the descending triangle floor. No equivalent technical level exists in the component NZD/USD or USD/JPY pairs that would generate this pair-specific systematic flow.

signposts.confirms_if: Rises above 2.50% — signals further BoJ normalisation pricing, compressing the NZ-Japan 10Y spread below +242bp and reducing NZD/JPY carry attraction
signposts.invalidates_if: Falls below 2.00% — signals risk-off flight to JGB safety or BoJ pause, removing the rate convergence catalyst
signposts.bilateral_importance: Rising JGB yields directly narrow the NZ-Japan 10Y spread (currently +242bp), reducing NZD/JPY's carry attractiveness for both institutional and Japanese retail participants. The yield is currently at its highest since 1999 and is the primary market signal of BoJ normalisation progress. For this specific pair, JGB yield direction is more relevant than NZ yield direction because the BoJ's policy trajectory is the active variable while the RBNZ is frozen.

signposts.confirms_if: Falls below $3,400/MT — removes the structural NZD commodity floor and compounds stagflation weakness
signposts.invalidates_if: Rises above $4,000/MT — activates dairy tailwind that dominates the bearish thesis and could trigger NZD short squeeze
signposts.bilateral_importance: Dairy is NZD's principal export commodity and a purely NZD-specific driver with no JPY offset — dairy price changes create clean directional NZD/JPY moves without the bilateral netting that applies to oil. The GDT auction's fortnightly schedule creates predictable NZD catalyst points. The current 18% YTD rally is the primary reason the NZD has not sold off more aggressively against JPY despite the stagflationary backdrop.

signposts.confirms_if: Stays above 25 — sustained risk-off environment where NZD/JPY's high beta ensures continued pair downside pressure
signposts.invalidates_if: Falls below 18 — risk normalisation would restart carry accumulation in NZD/JPY and compress the risk premium
signposts.bilateral_importance: NZD/JPY is one of the highest-beta G10 pairs to VIX — estimated beta of -0.4 to -0.6 at current levels (suppressed by energy channel). Moves of 5+ VIX points translate to 200+ pip NZD/JPY moves historically. As a pure risk-proxy pair, NZD/JPY responds to VIX more directly than most crosses where the safe-haven/risk-on dynamic is diluted by other factors. The current suppression of JPY's safe-haven channel (due to energy import costs) means the effective beta is lower than historical, but VIX normalisation would restore full beta exposure.

signposts.confirms_if: Falls below 30 — NZ confidence collapse deepens the stagflation narrative, increases probability of NZ recession, and reinforces RBNZ paralysis
signposts.invalidates_if: Recovers above 50 — pre-conflict recovery reasserts, suggesting NZ economy more resilient than feared and shifting RBNZ toward earlier hikes
signposts.bilateral_importance: ANZ Business Confidence is the most forward-looking NZ economic indicator and directly affects NZD sentiment and the RBNZ's policy calculus. The sharp March collapse (59→33) is one of the primary reasons the NZD dossier is bearish — further deterioration would lock in RBNZ inaction for longer. A recovery toward 50+ would shift the bilateral thesis by narrowing the guidance divergence between the BoJ (hiking) and RBNZ (frozen), which is currently one of the widest in years.

assumptions: PPP estimates are derived from dossier-provided OECD data: NZD is ~14% undervalued vs JPY, but this reflects both NZD cheapness and extreme JPY cheapness (BIS REER at all-time lows) — the pair-level PPP signal is muted and not used as a directional indicator.

Carry calculations use the policy rate differential of +150bp (RBNZ 2.25% vs BoJ 0.75%). Exact forward points are estimated from this differential; live market forward quotes were not accessible and the +11/+32/+55 pip estimates at 1M/3M/6M are approximations.

Implied volatility for NZD/JPY is estimated at 11–13% (1M) based on dossier-provided individual currency IV levels (NZD/USD ~14%, USD/JPY ~9.76%) and cross-pair construction methodology. Exact NZD/JPY options data was not available from free sources — these are calibrated estimates.

Risk reversal skew for NZD/JPY is estimated as mildly negative based on constituent pair risk reversals (NZD puts bid against USD; modest JPY put bid against USD). Exact NZD/JPY risk reversal data was behind terminal paywalls and is not sourced from live options markets.

Weight adjustments of +0.02 to Factor 3 (Risk Appetite) tactically and -0.02 to Factor 10 (USD Liquidity) at both horizons reflect NZD/JPY's status as a pure risk-proxy cross pair where risk sentiment has outsized relevance and direct USD funding is less impactful than for USD-denominated pairs.

Performance statistics, moving averages (50d at 92.72; 100d at 92.30; 200d at 90.00), and trend regime are derived from the provided Twelve Data daily OHLC dataset. Support and resistance levels were sourced from web research (Tickmill, EconoTimes, BabyPips) and cross-validated against the OHLC data.
BoJ hike probability at ~70% for April is drawn from the JPY dossier's OIS pricing data and treated as an external input to the NZD/JPY analysis rather than independently derived.

NZD CFTC percentile at 80th–90th is an estimate range given the dossier reports -41,742 contracts and the 3-year range context. The exact percentile was not precisely stated and has been populated at 85 as the midpoint of the reported range.
The NZD/JPY 52-week range percentile of 78% is calculated as (91.50 - 79.81) / (94.86 - 79.81) = 11.69 / 15.05 = 77.7%, rounded to 78th percentile.

Japan bilateral trade figure of US$6.4bn and NZ exports to Japan of US$2.32bn are sourced from cited Wikipedia and Trading Economics sources respectively. Minor discrepancies may exist due to different base years or methodology.
